paragraphs.text: When their bread was used up and all the household began to suffer hunger, all the sons approached Jacob with the words: “Have pity on Simeon for the sake of Simeon’s children, and consent for several days not to see thy youngest son for the sake of Simeon’s wife, who is now without husband.” Since Jacob himself too endured hunger, he reluctantly let Benjamin go with them, and sent them off with blessings: “As I have been bereaved of Rachel, so I am bereaved also of Rachel’s sons.” Then Judah, by way of consoling his father, said: “If I bring him not to thee and set him before thee, then let me bear the blame to thee for ever” (Gen 43:9).

They took “of the best fruits of the land” (Gen 43:11): styrax (fragrant gum), frankincense, pistachio nuts, and other things; came to Egypt and appeared before Joseph. He commanded the steward of his house to bring the brothers into his house. They, alarmed at Joseph’s servants taking the burdens off their animals and carrying what they had brought into the house, said to one another: “Our father has been bereaved of Benjamin through us, and we ourselves shall not see our father’s face again. For deliberately the silver was placed in our sacks. Even if we are freed from the suspicion of being spies, they will take us as thieves and make us slaves. Let us therefore ourselves confess to the steward that silver was found in our sacks, before they examine what we have brought and accuse us of theft. And as by the coming of Benjamin we are delivered from suspicion of spying, so by the confession of our lips we shall be delivered from suspicion of theft.”

Coming up “to the man that was over Joseph’s house” (Gen 43:19), the brothers spoke to him: “When, after buying corn here, we returned and opened our sacks, behold, every man’s silver was in his sack (Gen 43:21); behold, we restore it to thee, because it is not just, together with the corn, to take also what the corn is worth.” The steward, observing the fear in which they were, encouraged them: “Peace be to you, fear not (Gen 43:23). You were brought hither not because of the silver, which I received, but we desire to see you in our house for the faithfulness now proven. You come in not to be condemned for what you have not done. On the contrary, our master has called you to be his guests and to eat his bread. Our master is righteous, and by the honor he will pay you this time he wishes to blot out from your memory the dishonor you bore the first time.”

Then “Joseph came home, and they brought him the gifts… and bowed down before him” with trembling. “And he asked them of their welfare.” And they took heart. He asked also of their father: “Is he yet alive?” And they were comforted. He asked also of their brother: “Is this he?” And he blessed Benjamin and said: “God be gracious unto thee, my son” (Gen 43:26–27, 29). And all their fear vanished. Joseph spoke the blessing to his brother in the Egyptian tongue, and the interpreter conveyed it to the brothers.

“Joseph’s bowels did yearn upon his brother: and… he entered into his chamber, and wept,” at the time he was preparing a joyful feast for his brothers. During the meal Joseph reclined apart, the Egyptians reclined also apart, and to Joseph’s brothers places were appointed to recline apart (Gen 43:30, 32). Joseph, as if divining by his cup, assigned the brothers their places by seniority: to the elder was given the higher place, and to the younger the lower.

It is wonderful that the brothers did not recognize Joseph: when he supplied them with everything needful for the journey, when he restored the silver for the corn bought the first time, when he bound Simeon and ordered Benjamin to be brought, when he asked about their aged father, when he treated them with contempt, when he brought them into his house and blessed Benjamin, and finally when he showed that he knew the names of each of them. Although Joseph’s appearance was assumed and they were misled by his greatness, yet they could have remembered his dreams. So if the brothers did not recognize him because of his greatness, his rank, his language, and his stern manner, all this was from God, who hid Joseph from them until his dreams should be fulfilled in those who had sold him with the intention of making those dreams false.
